problems.problem: Raising funding for 10x more ambitious Community Archive project
problems.description: The author explicitly states at the end of the post that they're raising funding for "a 10x more ambitious project" and requests introductions to specific funding organizations (Cosmos, ARIA, Astera, Renaissance Philanthropy). In "discovering the postrat canon in the community archive lab notes 4," they mention this as an active fundraising need.
evidence: PS: I'm raising for a 10x more ambitious project so if you work with or close to organizations like Cosmos, ARIA, Astera, Renaissance Philanthropy, I'd love to talk to you.
I realized 60% of my desire of seeing the postrat canon as a whole, and I feel like I'm fulfilling the work I described in my funding proposal
We become more powerful as we get more data
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is an explicit funding ask with specific organizations named. The author is actively seeking introductions and has a concrete fundraising need. A stranger offering relevant connections or funding advice would get immediate attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Fundraising cycles typically take 3-6 months, and the author is still building out the current phase of the project while planning the next expansion. This will remain a top priority until secured.
problems.source_post: discovering the postrat canon in the community archive lab notes 4

problems.description: The author is actively fundraising $100k to build the nooscope over 18 months, with Vitalik Buterin pledging to match $50k of donations 1:1. They've provided a detailed budget breakdown and explicitly ask readers to contribute. In 'building tooling to map how ideas spread', they state their minimum viable budget and stretch goals.
evidence: Our goal is to raise $100k. Vitalik Buterin DMed me pledging to 1:1 match $50k of donations.
$100k is really our minimum, and a stretch goal of $250k would enable us to hire Goat and another contributor full time
Contribute - Reach me at @exgenesis
Budget: $60k: Living expenses for @exgenesis for a year, full-time. $20k: Hire @iaimforgoat, our best contributor, part-time for a year.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author explicitly asks for contributions and provides contact info. They have a concrete funding target with a high-profile matching pledge. This is an active fundraising campaign with urgency—they need this money to execute their 18-month roadmap.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Fundraising campaigns typically have a defined window. With Vitalik's match already secured and a clear roadmap, they likely need to close this round within 3-6 months to start execution. Once funded (or pivoted), this specific ask ends.
problems.source_post: building tooling to map how ideas spread

problems.problem: Looking for collaborators working on woo-science translation and multiscale coordination
problems.description: In 'xiq manifesto v01', the author explicitly states they are researching concrete high-leverage interventions and seeking to either join or establish a community of practice working on complementary problems. They feel a gap in explicit object-level coordination and prioritization among people working on similar projects, and are actively reaching out to find resonant collaborators.
evidence: I am still researching and evaluating concrete high-leverage interventions to focus on. Ideally I'd be able to either join or establish a community of practice that is at a similar page and doing complementary work.

I know for a fact many people are working on resonant or overlapping projects, and the 'theory of everything' podcast sphere is already active. People with big ideas are already talking, but I still feel a gap in explicit object-level coordination and prioritization.

I'm looking for high-leverage ways to deepen or apply these ideas. I am also looking for others who resonate with my approach and are working on similar or complementary problems. If this is you, reach out, I always enjoy triangulating the hyperobject!
For now the best course of action I have is communicating where I'm coming from in this document and reaching out to people I resonate with who are working on similar things.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author has explicitly and repeatedly stated they are looking for collaborators and community, making this a clear, urgent ask. They wrote this entire manifesto in part to reach people they'd like to collaborate with. A stranger with relevant collaboration credentials would get immediate attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is central to the author's long-term project (~5 year timeline mentioned). They're at the beginning stages of community-building and project definition, so this need will persist until they successfully establish or join a working group.

problems.problem: Need support/funding for Community Archive project
problems.description: In 'the ai for collective sensemaking unconference', the author closes with an explicit ask for people to support Community Archive on opencollective and contribute data. This is positioned as critical infrastructure for applying AI to social data and building collective sensemaking tools. The author presented Community Archive and Epistemic Garden at the conference and is clearly actively seeking resources to sustain and grow this work.
evidence: If you resonate, I hope you will consider contributing your data to the community archive, or support it on opencollective.
I talked about the Community Archive and Epistemic Garden, and what we can do applying AI to social data.
I believe there are abundant public goods in applying AI to social data, and that we must be sovereign over the data we generate.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author makes an explicit ask for support at the end of the post. They're actively building this project, running conferences around it, and directly requesting funding/contributions. A stranger with relevant funding opportunities or partnership proposals would likely get a serious response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Community Archive is clearly a core ongoing project - the author is running conferences around it, has it set up on opencollective, and positions it as fundamental infrastructure. This is not a sprint but sustained work that will need resources for at least a year.
problems.source_post: the ai for collective sensemaking unconference

problems.problem: Getting high-value accounts to upload archives to Community Archive
problems.description: In 'upload to the community archive', the author describes the manual, labor-intensive work of personally reaching out to influential Twitter accounts to walk them through uploading their archives. This is positioned as 'the legwork' that follows the initial technical launch, and represents the current bottleneck to building value in the database.
evidence: Now comes the legwork: we're personally reaching out to old school and central accounts and walking them through requesting and uploading their archives.
If you have any questions or need support, please reach out to me on twitter
The plan for this website is to let you upload, and to serve as a dashboard for the effort of gathering community archive
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author explicitly describes this as their current priority after launching the technical infrastructure. They're actively doing outreach and offering personal support. A stranger with insights on growth tactics, influencer outreach strategies, or connections to high-value accounts would likely get a meaningful response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a fundamental growth challenge for a newly launched public database. The author will need to continue recruiting high-value contributors for at least several months to reach critical mass. The project is past the technical MVP stage and now in growth mode.

problems.problem: Building connections between AI/sensemaking and cryptography/data liberation communities
problems.description: In 'the ai for collective sensemaking unconference', the author emphasizes they were 'especially pleased with connecting two clusters: people coming more from an AI and collective sensemaking background, and people coming from the side of protocols, cryptography, and data liberation.' They call this 'a match made in heaven' and highlight TEEs as critical for multiplayer coordination apps, suggesting active work to bridge these communities.
evidence: I was especially pleased with connecting two clusters: people coming more from an AI and collective sensemaking background, and people coming from the side of protocols, cryptography, and data liberation. I think it's a match made in heaven.

It seems like TEEs will be critical for multiplayer coordination apps that work on sensitive data so I was very excited to connect the TEE community and the rest of the AI for collective sensemaking space.
As far as I know, it was the first time many of these people were meeting their peers in person, with opportunities to discuss and coordinate!
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author just hosted a conference specifically to bridge these communities and expressed particular excitement about this connection. They're clearly invested in fostering this ecosystem. A stranger with relevant technical expertise or connections spanning both domains would likely get attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The unconference just happened (October 26), and the author is clearly in momentum-building mode for this emerging field. However, this is more about ecosystem-building than a specific technical blocker, so the urgency may moderate after initial connections are established.
problems.source_post: the ai for collective sensemaking unconference

problems.problem: Scaling Community Archive data beyond 250 accounts
problems.description: The author needs to grow the Twitter Community Archive dataset and expand to new data sources. In 'building tooling to map how ideas spread', they outline scaling needs: serving more communities, implementing real-time data collection via browser extension, and integrating Bluesky data to build sufficient dataset for memetics research.
evidence: The archive is an open database and API with 16M tweets from the volunteered archives of ~250 accounts.
It can scale: Serve more communities. The software is open-source, so anyone can start a database for their community.
Real time data firehose via our browser extension, avoiding the laborious upload process.
By the end, we want to help at least 2 other communities to set up their own separate archives (proving that scaling pathway), establish partners to integrate with bluesky
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is explicitly part of their 18-month roadmap goals. They need partnerships, community adoption, and technical implementation for real-time data. A stranger offering relevant expertise in community building, Bluesky integration, or data pipeline scaling would get serious attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a core infrastructure challenge for their 18-month project timeline. The data scaling problem will persist throughout the nooscope development period, as better tools require more and better data.
problems.source_post: building tooling to map how ideas spread

problems.problem: Need better data filtering to improve Community Archive signal quality
problems.description: The author identifies specific quality control issues in "discovering the postrat canon in the community archive lab notes 4": irrelevant tweets appearing in strands, missing important accounts, and the need to filter for opted-in users versus all streaming data. They note this is blocking better analysis quality.
evidence: I also see some tweets highlighted sometimes that aren't especially connected. Like this one with a culture war tone from someone who shares only 17 mutuals with me.
It could be a good time to filter only for users who've opted-in to being streamed. It would give us a denser higher signal core.
Long-term with adoption, the strategy of relying on the self-selection of users for quality will stop working and we'll want to do some community-finding to filter our analysis.
I would also note that we are missing accounts I would consider important in the scene, like vgr, nickcammaratta, aella_girl, tszzl, liminal_warmth, michelcurzi, deepfates, selentelechia.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a real technical problem they're wrestling with and have identified concrete issues. However, they seem to have clear ideas about solutions (filtering opted-in users, community-finding algorithms). It's not blocking progress but would improve quality. A stranger with relevant technical expertise might get a read but not necessarily a reply.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is an ongoing infrastructure problem that will become more pressing as adoption grows. The author has identified it and will likely address it in the next 3-4 months as part of iterating on the platform.
problems.source_post: discovering the postrat canon in the community archive lab notes 4

problems.problem: Need better LLM prompting to identify narrative strands from semantic clusters
problems.description: In 'deriving strands of discourse from a single tweet lab notes 3,' the authors tried using LLMs (specifically Gemini 3) to identify narrative strands within semantic clusters of tweets, but got C+ to B- quality results with hallucinations and misunderstandings. They acknowledge needing to try different LLMs or refine their approach, but this is blocking their ability to autonomously find stories at scale.
evidence: Upon inspecting more clusters, we weren't super satisfied with the results. At least with our naive prompt, Gemini 3 hallucinated lots of references and misunderstood jokes as more significant than they were.
I would say the LLM output was somewhere between a C+ and B-, which motivated us to try a different approach before trying to refine this one.
We're analyzing these stories manually to understand what typical strands of narrative are like, so we can better prompt LLMs to find them autonomously.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a real technical problem blocking their research pipeline, and they explicitly state they need to improve their prompting approach. However, they've already found a workaround (analyzing from single tweets instead of clusters) and are making progress, so while they'd read an email about this carefully, it's not an urgent blocker requiring immediate external help.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The authors are actively researching narrative detection methods and explicitly plan to return to improving LLM-based strand identification after manual analysis. This is a core technical component of their 'nooscope' tooling project, suggesting it will remain relevant for several months as they iterate.
problems.source_post: deriving strands of discourse from a single tweet lab notes 3

problems.problem: LLM misinterpreting high-context tweets in narrative strand extraction
problems.description: In "Bangers! are highly quoted tweets | lab notes #2", the authors built a pipeline to extract narrative strands from tweet clusters, but found that Gemini 3 Pro was "not quite bad at interpreting tweets" because tweets are "high context, often half-jokes and half-serious" and the model "tends to take them as factual statements." The LLM-generated descriptions didn't match the actual tweet content, undermining the core technical approach.
evidence: The strands picked up by Gemini 3 Pro were interesting themes (e.g. picking up on the rise of the fetters), but when digging into the LLM-generated description of the strand narrative, the tweets quoted didn't support the claims.
Tweets are high context, often half-jokes and half-serious, and Gemini tends to take them as factual statements.
This observation reflected the vagueness of our prompt: what was a narrative strand, really?
In general, this diminished our excitement with our naive pipeline, and decided not to scale up to apply it to the whole CA.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a real technical blocker they encountered and haven't fully solved. They abandoned their initial pipeline but plan to retry next week with a different approach. They acknowledge potential solutions (prompt tweaks, scaffolding, better ontology) but haven't implemented them. A stranger with relevant expertise might get attention, but it's one of several problems they're juggling.
problems.persistence_reasoning: They explicitly say they're "hoping to refocus on finding narrative strands" next week and want to "make a second pass" at the problem. This is an active technical challenge in an early-stage project, likely to persist for a few months as they iterate.
problems.source_post: bangers are highly quoted tweets lab notes 2

problems.problem: Building applications on top of Community Archive API
problems.description: In 'upload to the community archive', the author lists many potential applications (semantic search, books from tweets, dashboards, digital anthropology) but notes the current functionality is limited to basic search. They explicitly state they're 'hoping other neat and complex applications will come from others building on this data, or from our own side-projects.'
evidence: What can I do with it now? For now, only search across all the archives.
We're hoping other neat and complex applications will come from others building on this data, or from our own side-projects.
Enable cool applications like: Semantic search over tweets, Producing books from tweet collections, Dashboards of your interests and takes, LLM-based digital anthropology
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author has built the infrastructure and is clearly interested in applications, but this reads more aspirational than urgent. They list many ideas but haven't prioritized which to build. A stranger offering to build something specific might get attention, but it's not as clearly blocked as the archive-gathering effort.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a longer-term opportunity that will remain relevant as the archive grows. The author positions this as ongoing ('side-projects') rather than immediate. As more archives are collected, the value and urgency of building applications will increase over time.

problems.problem: Need validation method for big-picture sensemaking comparative advantage
problems.description: In 'xiq manifesto v01', the author identifies big-picture sensemaking as a potential comparative advantage but admits the evidence isn't strong. They specifically mention wanting to practice forecasting to establish a track record, and express difficulty gauging progress in their flow/grace cultivation without teachers or belonging to a single tradition.
evidence: My evidence for this isn't that strong, and one thing I'd like to do is practice forecasting and see if I can establish a good record.
However, it's hard to gauge progress in this sort of thing, especially without teachers or belonging to a single tradition.
I think one of my comparative advantages is relatively rigorous big-picture sensemaking
One way to get some real feedback on this would be to take up forecasting which I kind of want to do.
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author explicitly identifies this as something they want to do and need (validation/feedback), but it's positioned as secondary to their main collaboration search. It's a real blocker for career strategy clarity, but they have workarounds (continuing without validation). A stranger offering a concrete solution would get a careful read.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a near-term skill development goal that could be addressed relatively quickly through forecasting practice or finding mentors. It's important for career direction but not the core multi-year project.

problems.problem: Building autonomous tools to detect narrative strands in community data at scale
problems.description: In 'deriving strands of discourse from a single tweet lab notes 3,' the authors want to build tools that autonomously find narrative strands in Twitter community data, but currently must analyze stories manually. They're developing computational methods iteratively but need to scale beyond manual curation to make their 'nooscope' vision practical.
evidence: We're analyzing these stories manually to understand what typical strands of narrative are like, so we can better prompt LLMs to find them autonomously.
We want to build tools that let communities understand themselves and the world and make better decisions towards their own pursuits of good, truth, and beauty.
We want to build tools that feel like pointing a telescope at the plane of ideas and collective thought. A 'nooscope' into the 'noosphere'.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is their core research project and they're actively working on it, but it's a longer-term vision rather than an immediate blocker. They're making iterative progress with their current pipeline and haven't issued an explicit call for help. A stranger's email would be read with interest but not urgency.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is described as a multi-phase research project ('lab notes #3' suggests ongoing series) with a long-term vision of building community tools. The authors are still in the exploratory phase, manually analyzing to inform future automation, indicating this will be an active problem for at least a year.
problems.source_post: deriving strands of discourse from a single tweet lab notes 3

problems.problem: Finding compelling value proposition for TPOT powerusers who already know the archive
problems.description: In "Bangers! are highly quoted tweets | lab notes #2", the team spent 20-30% then 80% of week 2 brainstorming their mission, wrestling with the question: "If we are building tools for TPOT powerusers, they are the people that already know best the content from the community archive... What new and useful interface to this information can we provide to these powerusers?" This strategic uncertainty is consuming increasing amounts of their working time.
evidence: Throughout the week, we also spend ~ 20-30% of the time brainstorming about the general mission of our project, trying to see if we can come up with a concrete and ambitious pitch we could get excited by. By the last days, this ratio rose to ~ 80%

If we are building tools for TPOT powerusers, they are the people that already know best the content from the community archive, maybe even produced a fair bit of its canon. What new and useful interface to this information can we provide to these powerusers?
What should be the desiderata for a nooscope?
If there were a FRO about large scale online coordination, what should be its metric?
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is a live strategic question that's consuming significant time (up to 80% by end of week 2), but it's still somewhat exploratory and philosophical. The questions are genuine but broad. A stranger would need very specific insights about TPOT poweruser needs or nooscope design to get meaningful attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is an early-stage project (only two weeks in) struggling with product-market fit and vision. Strategic questions like this typically persist for months in early projects, especially when the team is splitting time between building and strategizing.
problems.source_post: bangers are highly quoted tweets lab notes 2

problems.problem: Need curated examples and case studies to communicate FEP value effectively
problems.description: In "free energy odyssey," the author identifies that to do an "amazing job at communicating the value" of the FEP and active inference, they need "a curated list of examples" and "a few case studies of looking at unknown systems through the FEP." They've written multiple incomplete drafts (80% of a "low road" explainer, 80% of a technical explainer) but haven't been satisfied with any approach, suggesting this is a blocking issue for completing their explanatory work.
evidence: I think to do an amazing job at communicating the value I see in the FEP and active inference as frames, I'd want a curated list of examples. As well as a few 'case studies' of looking at unknown systems through the FEP to make sense of them.

I think I could start gathering examples by just reading a bunch of applications of active inference to different fields like morphogenesis and social systems and art, but that sounds like a lot of work.
Despite my resistance to gather examples, I'm glad that I got to dive deep on some pretty fundamental conceptual apparatus
problems.email_open_reasoning: While the author explicitly identifies needing examples and case studies, they also express 'resistance to gather examples' and acknowledge they're 'open to realizing I actually don't get that much more value from the lens.' This suggests it's more of an intellectual interest than an urgent need. They've already moved past the phase of actively trying to write explainers.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The author has already spent significant time on this (multiple 80% drafts) and is now at a reflective conclusion phase rather than active work phase. The lack of urgency ('that sounds like a lot of work,' 'not burning a very big hole in my curiosity') suggests they may not actively pursue this much longer.
